one_line_takeaway: Two tiny models (one safe, one unsafe) can hijack the decoding of a much larger aligned LLM in a single forward pass, pushing jailbreak success above 99% and exposing that current open-source safety alignment is shallow and largely confined to the first few generated tokens.
key_finding: The weak-to-strong attack drives misalignment on aligned Llama2 models to near-perfect rates: 99.4% ASR on AdvBench and 99.0% on MaliciousInstruct for Llama2-13B, and 99.2%/100.0% for Llama2-70B (Table 2), versus the strong baselines GCG (25.4%/26.0% on 13B; 56.2%/79.0% on 70B) and generation-exploitation "Best Top-K/Top-p/Temp" (up to ~95% on 13B but far lower harmfulness). Harmfulness roughly doubles: attacked 70B reaches Harm Score 3.90 / GPT-4 4.07 on AdvBench vs. ~1.1-1.8 for decoding baselines. The attack beats even direct adversarial fine-tuning of the target (Table 3: 99.4 vs 93.7 ASR on Llama2-13B AdvBench) and generalizes across three model families, to SALAD-Bench/SORRY-Bench (>96% ASR), and zero-shot to Chinese (94.5%) and French (95.0%). A pruned Sheared-LLaMA-1.3B (only 3.7% of the victim's parameters) still jailbreaks Llama2-70B at 74.0% ASR on AdvBench. The proposed gradient-ascent defense reduces generation-exploitation attack ASR by ~20-41% (Figure 5) but only reduces the paper's own weak-to-strong attack ASR by ~5-10% (5.1% and 10.2% shown), while leaving capability nearly intact (TruthfulQA -0.04; GSM8K ~unchanged).
core_contribution: The paper introduces "weak-to-strong jailbreaking," an inference-time attack that makes a large safety-aligned open-source LLM produce harmful text by steering its decoding with two much smaller models. The attack rests on the paper's own empirical observation (Section 3.1, Figures 1-2) that aligned and jailbroken models differ mainly in their initial decoding tokens: KL divergence between safe and unsafe next-token distributions is high early and decays over decoding steps, and top-10 token overlap between safe/unsafe models exceeds 50% and rises with prefix length — evidence that safety alignment is "shallow." Exploiting this, the attack modifies the strong safe target M+ token distribution via log-probability algebra using a weak safe reference M- and a weak unsafe model M-hat: intuitively Safe-70B + alpha*(Unsafe-7B - Safe-7B), where alpha is an amplification factor. This requires only one forward pass through the target per token, no gradient optimization of or weight modification to the target, and no prompt engineering; it requires that the strong and weak models share a vocabulary. The paper also proposes an initial gradient-ascent defense. Related-work methods (GCG, PAIR, AutoDAN, DExperts, contrastive/speculative decoding, proxy-tuning) are cited as prior/contrasting work, not claimed as this paper's contribution — DExperts-style log-prob algebra is explicitly noted as originating in controlled-generation/detoxification work, and the paper's novelty is repurposing decoding-time manipulation as a jailbreak on very large aligned models.
threat_model: White-box / open-weights setting: the adversary has access to the large aligned target model's output token distributions (logits) and can run two small models sharing the target's vocabulary — a weak safe reference model and a weak unsafe model. The unsafe small model is obtained cheaply, either by adversarially fine-tuning a 7B model on ~100 harmful examples or by using the un-aligned base model directly. The adversary does NOT need to modify the target's weights, optimize prompts, or run backpropagation; the attack needs only one forward pass through the target per generated token (Table 1 threat-model comparison). Adversary goal: elicit harmful/unethical content from the aligned large model. The paper notes the attack could theoretically extend to closed-source models if partial logits are exposed (or recovered via logit-extraction), but this is left unverified.
limitations: By the authors' own account: (1) the attack requires access to output logits/token distributions, restricting applicability to open-source models — closed-source effectiveness is called "theoretically feasible but unverified." (2) It requires the strong and weak models to share a vocabulary (token-alignment workarounds are cited but not demonstrated here). (3) It incurs extra compute for the two auxiliary models (~20% overhead, reducible to ~3.7% with pruned models). (4) The proposed gradient-ascent defense is explicitly "an initial attempt" and is weak against the very attack introduced — it cuts the weak-to-strong ASR by only ~5-10%, far less than the 20-40% drop it achieves against generation-exploitation attacks, so no effective defense is demonstrated. Not independently verified but worth flagging: harmfulness scoring relies heavily on an automated reward model and GPT-4 judge (human MTurk evaluation is mentioned but its numbers are in the appendix, not the extracted main text).

datasets_used: 200 English questions translated to Chinese and French (multilingual eval)
quantitative_results: Weak-to-Strong ASR: Llama2-13B 99.4% AdvBench / 99.0% MaliciousInstruct; Llama2-70B 99.2% / 100.0% (Table 2)
Harm/GPT-4 scores ~2x baselines: attacked Llama2-70B Harm 3.90 / GPT-4 4.07 on AdvBench vs GCG 3.06/3.15 and decoding baselines ~1.1-1.8
Beats direct adversarial fine-tuning of target: 99.4 vs 93.7 ASR (Llama2-13B AdvBench, Table 3)
Cross-family >99% ASR: Vicuna-13B 100.0%, Baichuan2-13B 99.2%, InternLM-20B 100.0% on AdvBench (Table 6)
Multilingual zero-shot: Chinese 94.5% ASR / Harm 4.09, French 95.0% / 4.35 on Llama2-13B (Table 7)
Extreme weak model: Sheared-LLaMA-1.3B (3.7% of victim params) jailbreaks Llama2-70B at 74.0% ASR on AdvBench
SALAD-Bench / SORRY-Bench: Attack-70B 97.2% / 97.1% ASR vs Safe-13B 13.9% / 12.8% (Table 5)
Amplification factor alpha (1.0->1.5) raises both ASR and harm score (Fig 4: 70B 98.3%->99.2%, harm 3.69->3.90)
Attack needs only 1 forward pass and 0 backward passes vs GCG ~10^3 forward / ~10^2 backward (Table 1)
Low output overlap with small unsafe model: BLEU < 0.3 (Table 4), indicating strong model is not merely copying the weak one
Defense (100-step gradient ascent, 200 harmful pairs): cuts generation-exploitation ASR by ~20-41% but weak-to-strong ASR by only ~5.1-10.2% (Fig 5); TruthfulQA drop 0.04, GSM8K ~unchanged (Table 9)
baselines_compared: Adversarial Decoding / generation exploitation - Best Temp, Best Top-K, Best Top-p (Huang et al., 2023)
Adversarial Fine-tuning (Yang et al., 2023; Qi et al., 2023)
